title: Mary's Song
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Mary’s Song - Luke 1_39-56 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The Visitation: Mary Goes to Elizabeth
subpoints: Mary, newly pregnant with Jesus, travels to visit her cousin Elizabeth
Elizabeth, filled with the Holy Spirit, blesses Mary and acknowledges the Lord
John the Baptist leaps in the womb at the presence of the incarnate Son of God
The Holy Spirit confirms the miracle before any words are exchanged

outline.title: The Magnificat: Mary Magnifies the Lord
subpoints: Mary's soul magnifies the Lord and her spirit rejoices in God her Savior
Mary acknowledges her lowliness and yet God has looked upon her with favor
All generations will call her blessed — not because of her merit but because of God's grace
The Holy One who is to be born of her is the fulfillment of God's eternal purposes

outline.title: The Character of God Displayed in Redemption
subpoints: God's mercy extends from generation to generation to those who fear Him
God scatters the proud, pulls down the mighty, exalts the humble — the great reversal
He fills the hungry with good things and sends the rich away empty
This reversal is not merely social but spiritual — the gospel upends human expectations

outline.title: Covenant Faithfulness: The Song Rooted in God's Promises to Israel
subpoints: God has helped His servant Israel, in remembrance of His mercy
The promise to Abraham and his offspring is fulfilled in the coming of Christ
Mary's song echoes the song of Hannah (1 Samuel 2:1-10) — God vindicates the humble
The Magnificat shows that Mary understood the theological meaning of what was happening in her womb

outline.title: Application: The Posture of the Redeemed
subpoints: Mary's response to the most remarkable event in history is worship, not self-promotion
Humility before God and dependence on His grace is the mark of all who are saved
We are to magnify God — not to make Him bigger, but to see Him as He truly is
The coming of Christ into the world calls forth the same response from us: praise

summary:
In this sermon on Luke 1:39-56, Pastor Lyro expounds the Magnificat — the song of Mary upon her visit to her cousin Elizabeth. The passage begins with the visitation, in which Mary, newly pregnant with the Lord Jesus, travels to see Elizabeth, who is six months pregnant with John the Baptist. When Elizabeth hears Mary's greeting, John leaps in the womb and Elizabeth is filled with the Holy Spirit, pronouncing a blessing on Mary as the mother of her Lord. The Holy Spirit confirms the miracle before any natural explanation can be offered.

Mary's response to all of this is not self-exaltation but a remarkable hymn of praise — the Magnificat. She magnifies the Lord and rejoices in God her Savior, acknowledging her own lowliness and God's sovereign favor upon her. She does not draw attention to herself but to the God who has done great things. The sermon emphasizes that the word 'magnify' does not mean to make God bigger, but to see Him as He truly is — and Mary, overwhelmed by grace, does exactly that.

The Magnificat then broadens into a meditation on the character and purposes of God in redemption: His mercy from generation to generation, the great reversal by which the proud are scattered and the humble exalted, the hungry filled and the rich sent away empty. This is not merely social commentary but a description of the gospel itself, which overturns all human expectations of who is great and who is small. The song closes with Mary's affirmation that in the coming of Jesus, God has kept faith with His servant Israel and remembered His promise to Abraham. Pastor Lyro draws connections to the Song of Hannah in 1 Samuel 2, showing that the Magnificat stands in a long tradition of God's people praising Him for vindicating the humble. The sermon calls the congregation to the same posture as Mary: humility, dependence on grace, and worship.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Luke 1:39-56

*[Full transcript text for this sermon is drawn from the source file: Mary's Song - Luke 1_39-56 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt]*

Now in those days Mary arose and went with haste into the hill country, to a town in Judah, and she entered the house of Zechariah and greeted Elizabeth. And when Elizabeth heard the greeting of Mary, the baby leaped in her womb. And Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit, and she exclaimed with a loud cry, "Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb! And why is this granted to me that the mother of my Lord should come to me? For behold, when the sound of your greeting came to my ears, the baby in my womb leaped for joy. And blessed is she who believed that there would be a fulfillment of what was spoken to her from the Lord."

And Mary said:

"My soul magnifies the Lord,
and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior,
for he has looked on the humble estate of his servant.
For behold, from now on all generations will call me blessed;
for he who is mighty has done great things for me,
and holy is his name.
And his mercy is for those who fear him
from generation to generation.
He has shown strength with his arm;
he has scattered the proud in the thoughts of their hearts;
he has brought down the mighty from their thrones
and exalted those of humble estate;
he has filled the hungry with good things,
and the rich he has sent away empty.
He has helped his servant Israel,
in remembrance of his mercy,
as he spoke to our fathers,
to Abraham and to his offspring forever."

And Mary remained with her about three months and returned to her home.

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together.

## The Visitation

Mary, newly pregnant with Jesus — the Son of God, the Second Person of the eternal Trinity, incarnate in her womb by the Holy Spirit — travels with haste to visit her cousin Elizabeth. Elizabeth is six months pregnant with John the Baptist, the forerunner of the Messiah. There is in this visit a convergence of two extraordinary pregnancies, both the work of the Holy Spirit, both announced by the angel Gabriel, both signs that the long-awaited day of the Lord is at hand.

When Elizabeth hears Mary's greeting — before Mary can say a word about what has happened to her — the baby in Elizabeth's womb leaps, and Elizabeth is filled with the Holy Spirit. The Spirit confirms the miracle. Elizabeth blesses Mary among all women and blesses the fruit of her womb. She asks: "Why is this granted to me that the mother of my Lord should come to me?" The word "Lord" here is the same word used throughout the Septuagint — the Greek Old Testament — for Yahweh. Elizabeth is calling the unborn child in Mary's womb her Lord. She recognizes — by the Spirit — who this is.

And she adds: "Blessed is she who believed that there would be a fulfillment of what was spoken to her from the Lord." Mary's faith — her trust in the promise of God delivered by the angel — is commended. She believed when it was humanly impossible. That is the posture of faith.

## The Magnificat: Mary Magnifies the Lord

And then Mary sings. What a song. The church has treasured this song for two thousand years, calling it the Magnificat — from its first word in Latin. It is one of the great theological hymns of the Scriptures, and it tells us something profound about who Mary was. Mary was a young woman who had saturated herself in the Hebrew Scriptures — particularly the Psalms and the Prophets — so much so that when the greatest moment of her life arrived, she responded in the language of Scripture. This is not a spontaneous outburst of raw emotion; it is a carefully structured theological hymn.

"My soul magnifies the Lord, and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior."

The word "magnifies" does not mean to make God larger than He is. You cannot make God bigger than He already is. To magnify the Lord means to show Him, to display Him, to set Him forth so that others can see how great He truly is. Mary's life — and particularly this moment — is a magnifying glass that makes God's greatness visible.

Notice: she rejoices in God her *Savior*. Mary knows she needs a Savior. She is not her own Savior. The one who is most blessed among women still needs the grace of God for her own salvation. This immediately rules out any notion that Mary is a co-redemptrix or a mediator. She is the recipient of grace, not its source.

"For He has looked on the humble estate of His servant. For behold, from now on all generations will call me blessed; for He who is mighty has done great things for me, and holy is His name."

God looked upon Mary's lowliness. Not her virtue — her lowliness. That is grace. And from that grace flows the remarkable result: all generations will call her blessed. Not because she earned it. Not because of some inherent quality. Because God in His grace chose her. All the blessing flows from Him: "He who is mighty has done great things for me, and holy is His name."

## The Character of God in Redemption

"And His mercy is for those who fear Him from generation to generation."

God's mercy is not a momentary exception. It extends from generation to generation — to those who fear Him. That is the covenant God — the God who keeps His promises down through the ages, who is the same yesterday, today, and forever.

"He has shown strength with His arm; He has scattered the proud in the thoughts of their hearts; He has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate; He has filled the hungry with good things, and the rich He has sent away empty."

This is the great reversal — what God does in the economy of redemption. The proud are scattered. The mighty are brought down. The humble are exalted. The hungry are filled. The rich are sent away empty.

Now this is not simply a social manifesto. Mary is not announcing a political revolution. She is describing the character of God in the gospel. The gospel turns all human expectations upside down. Who does God exalt? Not the powerful, not the self-sufficient, not those who think they have earned His favor. He exalts the humble. He fills the hungry. He is the God of those who know they have nothing and can do nothing — who come to Him with empty hands, trusting only in His grace.

The song of Mary echoes the song of Hannah in 1 Samuel 2. Hannah was barren, humiliated, and desperate — and God opened her womb and gave her Samuel. And she sang: "The Lord kills and makes alive; He brings down to Sheol and raises up. The Lord makes poor and makes rich; He brings low and He exalts. He raises up the poor from the dust; He lifts the needy from the ash heap." Same God. Same pattern. Same grace.

## Covenant Faithfulness

"He has helped His servant Israel, in remembrance of His mercy, as He spoke to our fathers, to Abraham and to his offspring forever."

And here we see the deep roots of what is happening. The coming of Jesus into the world is not a new plan devised to fix a problem God didn't anticipate. It is the fulfillment of a promise God made to Abraham — that in his offspring all the nations of the earth would be blessed (Genesis 17). The whole of Israelite history has been the unfolding of that promise. And now, in the womb of this young woman from Galilee, the promise is about to be definitively fulfilled.

"To Abraham and to his offspring forever" — this is an eternal covenant. And Jesus is the offspring in whom all the covenant promises find their Yes and Amen.

## Application: The Posture of the Redeemed

Mary's response to the most remarkable event in the history of the world is not self-promotion, not pride, not a demand to be recognized. It is worship. Her soul magnifies the Lord. Her spirit rejoices in God her Savior.

Brothers and sisters, this is the posture of all who are redeemed. Humility before God. Dependence on His grace. Recognition that every good thing — every blessing, every hope, every joy — flows from Him. "He has done great things for me, and holy is His name."

The coming of Christ into the world calls forth from us the same response it called forth from Mary: praise. Not praise of ourselves. Not pride in our position. Praise of the God who looked upon our humble estate and, for no reason found in us, sent His Son to be our Savior.

"My soul magnifies the Lord, and my spirit rejoices in God my Savior."

May that be true of us, by His grace.

Let us pray. Father, we thank You for the gift of Your Son. We thank You for the faith of Mary, who believed Your Word when it was humanly impossible. We thank You that You scatter the proud and exalt the humble — and we confess that we need to be humbled before You. We pray that our souls would truly magnify You — that those around us would see You more clearly because of how we live. We ask in Jesus' name. Amen.
